Sorry for the late answer. I didn't immediately see the problem, so I deferred looking closer at this.

The problem here is that your main thread doesn't do anything after it started the four actors, so it simply went of the cliff and shut down. Then you had noone to react to the `KeyboardInterrupt`.

The following example works nicely:

``` python
import logging
import signal
import time

import pykka
import pykka.debug


LOGGER = logging.getLogger(__name__)


class send_flow(pykka.ThreadingActor):
    pass


def main():
    logging.basicConfig(level=logging.DEBUG)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGUSR1, pykka.debug.log_thread_tracebacks)

    try:
        for i in range(0, 4):
            locals()['actor_{0}'.format(i)] = send_flow.start()
        while True:
            time.sleep(0.1)
    except KeyboardInterrupt:
        LOGGER.info("Stopping actors...")
        # Close all actors to stop blocking
        for i in pykka.ActorRegistry.get_all():
            LOGGER.info("Stopping Actor: " + i.actor_urn + " ...")
            i.stop()
            LOGGER.info("Stopped Actor: " + i.actor_urn)
        LOGGER.info("Stopped actors.")

if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

Now, the Pykka thread state debugger I added also works. The signal handling it uses depends on the main thread being alive and not blocked, ref https://www.pykka.org/en/latest/api/#pykka.debug.log_thread_tracebacks.

> I would like to configure ProhibitUnusedVariable (which is not enabled by default currently) to ignore variables named _.

It can be configured by `policies.ProhibitUnusedVariable.ignored_patterns` since [v0.4a1](https://github.com/Kuniwak/vint/releases/tag/v0.4a1). This is the example for ignoring `_` and `__` and so on:

```yaml
---
policies:
  ProhibitUnusedVariable:
    enabled: yes
    ignored_patterns:
      - '^_+$'
```

But now it is not default option. Because I do not know whether the meaning of `_` is widely agreed or not.

> This is already not the case since vint treats the enabled policy option in a special way.
> I've also would like to discuss this situation with you, but maybe in a separate PR later.

I think there are no special ways to define default values.
Or do you mean `"severity"`?


> And what exactly do you mean by 'context state of AST'?

I meant "context state of AST" as the state of AST-traversing.
Some classes have the state of AST-traversing. For example, `ProhibitEncodingOptionAfterScriptEncoding` have a stateful property that is `was_scriptencoding_found`.


> The problem with purely stateless policies is performance.

I think we should not do this to make better performance until performance issue opened; "The Second Rule of Program Optimization (for experts only!): Don't do it yet."

Indeed `define` is built in such a way that allows a single definition. You can use `load_definitions` and provide a line iterable. For example,

```python
>>> import pint
>>> ureg = pint.UnitRegistry()
>>> ureg.load_definitions('''
... us_dollar = [currency] = $
... sheet_unit = [material_sheet]
... part_unit = [cutout_part]
... '''.split('\n'))
>>> ureg('1$')
1
>>> ureg('1us_dollar')
<Quantity(1, 'us_dollar')>
>>> ureg('1sheet_unit')
<Quantity(1, 'sheet_unit')>
```

> Is there any advice/examples available of best practices for creating a cget recipe for a library that does not already use CMake?

There is a bunch of recipes [here](https://github.com/pfultz2/cget-recipes) which includes recipes for a lot of non-cmake projects, like [xz](https://github.com/pfultz2/cget-recipes/tree/master/recipes/xz) or [wgtdkp/juson](https://github.com/pfultz2/cget-recipes/tree/master/recipes/wgtdkp/juson).

> Is there perhaps a way I can "inject" a custom CMakeLists.txt file as part of the recipe? (Or is that thinking about things in the wrong way?)

Yes, the `-X` or `--cmake` specifies a CMakeLists.txt file to be "injected" into the project.

Projects need to provide downloadable sources, this is very common practice for projects with open-source distributions(all projects in Debian can be downloaded as source tarballs and built).

As a workaround for projects that dont provide downloadable sources, you will need to get the sources using whatever tool the project requires to get the sources and then point cget to that directory. 

Another option would be to create a recipe that would go through those steps as well. This will let you keep the one-liner in your install_dependencies.sh file. Unfortunately, you won't be able to take advantage of cget's download cache.

The bigger issue is that an open source project should *always* provide sources that can be downloaded through a web browser(or tools such as wget or curl). Protocols such as `http` and `https` are readily available with any tool. You should contact authors of projects that do not provide downloadable sources before doing any workarounds.

@semirook 
About the hash problem, i have got three ideas.

## idea1:
Trust single node and its fail-over, set lock key in master node and then use cluster `readonly` mode to read lock key from slave node (may redlock algorithm be used) to confirm sync between master and slave is successful(in this way the lock is available until master node and all its slaves are down)

## idea2:
Generate specific key according to lock key given by user according to simple rule, and then use `setslot` to make sure most of node has generated key, the rest part is the redlock's job. 

## idea3:
Similar to idea2, but directly generating key by rule which can make them hashed to different slot and different node(it is hard, and it is needed to make sure the same output is given with the same input by different client process)

All these ideas can not handle the situation if most of the lock keys are migrated.

It is worth noting that this limitation is not a limitation of Spotmop. It is due to how Spotify handles authentication. In many cases server-side (aka _Client Credentials flow_) authentication is sufficient. You can use this by not logging in to Spotify via Spotmop.

> The *Client Credentials* flow allows you to get an access token by supplying your client credentials (client ID and secret key). This flow is used in server-to-server authentication. Only endpoints that do not access user information can be accessed. Its advantage is that a higher rate limit is applied compared with requests to the Web API made without an access token.

However, for several Spotify API endpoints we require user-specific information that requires the _Authorization Code_ flow. This can not be performed server-side. For example _My Tracks_, _My Albums_, etc.

> The *Authorization Code* flow first gets a code then exchanges it for an access token and a refresh token. Since the exchange uses your client secret key, you should make that request server-side to keep the integrity of the key. An advantage of this flow is that you can use refresh tokens to extend the validity of the access token.

Hopefully this helps you understand the reason this is not currently viable. For more information, visit [Spotify Authorization GUide](https://developer.spotify.com/web-api/authorization-guide/).

I think it may be better if the one or more deviation files were listed under a separate option to make it very clear which two files are being compared. I would suggest that `--check-update-from` still only take a single parameter, rather than assume that he first file listed is the one to compare. This would looks more like:

```
pyang -p BLD_POLARIS_DEV_LATEST_20180706_002134 \
  --check-update-from BLD_V169_THROTTLE_LATEST_20180712_092155/openconfig-interfaces.yang \
  --check-update-from-deviation-module BLD_V169_THROTTLE_LATEST_20180712_092155/cisco-xe-openconfig-interfaces-deviation.yang" \

  --deviation-module BLD_POLARIS_DEV_LATEST_20180706_002134/cisco-xe-openconfig-interfaces-deviation.yang
```

Having `--check-update-from-deviation-module` would make it symmetric with just compiling a module with a specific deviation using the option `--deviation-module`, which can already be specified multiple times, so the same behavior you desire couple be expressed consistently.

Dictionary merge is in v_import_module().  I would make sure the top module's dictionary contains all submodule's dictionaries, and that each submodule dict only contains its own definitions.  Then the lookup functions would check local dict first, then parent's.  But just for 1.1 modules.

In YANG 1, a submodule cannot refer to its parent's definitions.  You need to put all such definitions in a separate submodule; let's say foo-common.  Then you let all other submodules include foo-common.

Furthermore when you use ``self.object = ObjectDef(self.object_definition, connection)`` the ObjectDef definition is built automatically from the server schema, so you don't have to use AttrDef at all.

The following code (adapted from yours) works for me:

```
from ldap3 import Connection, ObjectDef, AttrDef, Reader
MY_SERVER = 'ipa.demo1.freeipa.org'
MY_USER = 'uid=admin,cn=users,cn=accounts,dc=demo1,dc=freeipa,dc=org'
MY_PASSWORD = 'Secret123'
MY_BASE = 'cn=users,cn=accounts,dc=demo1,dc=freeipa,dc=org'

class Entry(object):
    def __init__(self, connection):
        self.connection = connection
        self.object = ObjectDef(self.object_definition, connection)
        self._reader = None

    @property
    def reader(self):
        if self._reader:
            return self._reader
        else:
            self._reader = Reader(self.connection, self.object, MY_BASE)
            return self._reader

class PersonEntry(Entry):
    object_definition = 'Person'

    def __init__(self, connection):
        super(PersonEntry, self).__init__(connection)
        #schema = ****

c = Connection(MY_SERVER, MY_USER, MY_PASSWORD)
c.bind()
p = PersonEntry(c)
p.reader.search()
print(p.reader)
print(p.reader[0])
```

Anyway, I'm not going to add an install target in cmake.  I used to have one but most people who use python haven't got any idea how to use the dlib.so file if it's not installed by the python package manager into dist-packages.  That is, they don't know what the python path is, or what dist-packages is, or what a .so file is, or any of that stuff.  They just know they type some magic command and then they can write `import dlib` and it works or doesn't.  I still get many confused questions from people who have multiple copies of python installed and they installed dlib in one python's dist-packages but then started using a different python where isn't not installed and they are confused.  Many of them don't seem to even understand what a filesystem is.

So I used to have a cmake install target that would copy the dlib.so file into the python_examples folder because that lets you just run the examples, since python always searches the current folder for modules.  That seems like the simplest possible example to show what's going on.  You got the .py example program and the dlib.so you are loading, *which is what the example is about*.  But like I was saying above, it turns out that most python users can't handle that.  I honestly think many of the people who email me don't understand what a filesystem is. 

Anyway, because of all that there is no way I'm giving python users more than one path to install dlib.  I get enough confused emails already. You have to generally assume that many python users are just learning to use a computer so things need to be as easy and 'this is the one and only path' as possible.

I have to say that even as an experienced CMake user new to dlib, it took me a few minutes to figure out the unconventional organization (two top-level CMake projects in one repository and neither one in the repository root). I actually think that a better and simpler fix than 378431e would be to just have one top-level CMakeLists.txt that builds everything by default (dlib and examples) but has an option to disable examples (e.g. with -Ddlib_BUILD_EXAMPLES=OFF). If that's something your open to discussing, I can put together a separate pull request that shows what I'm talking about. I can also include some fixes to properly export the dlib::dlib target.

Did you not see the build instructions in the [README](https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/README.txt) or the [web page](http://dlib.net/compile.html)?  I try to make it very easy.  For example, I want the dlib example [CMakeList.txt](http://dlib.net/examples/CMakeLists.txt.html) to be so simple even someone new to CMake will understand it immediately.

I'm certainly open to improving the build scripts, and adding things needed to support more CMake 3.0 features is cool (provided everything still works in 2.8.4).  However, I'm not sure I understand why you would want the top level CMake file to build the examples.  Wouldn't that make your external build scripts build them by default? I doubt people who are using ExternalProject would want that behavior.

Also, consider that I get questions [like this one](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/35574967/how-to-use-dlib-in-projects) pretty much every day.  So while it's definitely important to make dlib convenient for expert users, I want to make sure it stays accessible to beginners (to the extent that that is possible).  I also want to encourage users to statically compile dlib into their applications.  So that should be the default recommendation in the documentation.  I say this because, in my experience, very few developers, even professionals, understand issues like C++'s [one definition rule](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/One_Definition_Rule) or that [mixing runtimes on windows](http://siomsystems.com/mixing-visual-studio-versions/) is bad.  Currently, the setup in the dlib example CMakeList.txt file avoids any possibility of these issues arising.  Even despite this, these kinds of user errors are so frequent (but only from non-cmake users) I've even put stuff inside the code to try and detect ODR violations or people doing bad things like writing `#include <dlib/all/source.cpp>` in their files (their compiler will drop them here https://github.com/davisking/dlib/blob/master/dlib/dlib_basic_cpp_build_tutorial.txt). 

But all that said, yes, please do submit PRs that improve the user experience for experts as well and thanks for this PR :)

Consider a web service for face recognition. Each client gets it's own thread, something like this:
```
@run_async
def generate_response(request):
    photo = load_photo(request)  # IO, releases GIL
    photo = process_photo_with_pillow(photo)  # releases GIL
    person = recognize_face_with_dlib(photo)  # oops
    upload_result(photo, person)  # IO, releases GIL
```

So, if `generate_response` is running in separate thread using `threading` module, GIL is acquired just for little amount of time between the IO and C-calls. Except for the dlib which enforces all other user's threads to wait. This is a real example of project on that I'm currently working on and all other libraries (numpy, Pillow, requests) releases GIL, but not dlib. And I have to move all dlib calls to another process and think about communication and synchronization between processes...

The new dlib cmake scripts use cmake's target properties which propagate all this kind of dependency information automatically.  But you have to use `dlib::dlib` rather than `dlib` as the argument to `target_link_libraries` for some reason to get it to happen.

To show that this isn't all puppies and rainbows, consider a quadratic-cubic case (the implicitized polynomials will have degree 6, also the cubic is "invalid" because it has an isolated point at `(57, -63)`):

```
f0 = 2(2s + 8t^3 - 18t^2 + 9t - 2)
g0 = -8s^2 + 8s - 20t^3 + 48t^2 - 33t + 5
DF0 = [4, -6]
      [0,  0]
```

and then

```
f1 =                  f0 = 2(2s + 8t^3 - 18t^2 + 9t - 2)
g1 = grad(f0) x grad(g0) = 12(64st^2 - 96st + 24s - 52t^2 + 80t - 23)
DF1 = [  4,  -6]
      [-96, 144]
```

and then

```
f2 =                  f1 = 2(2s + 8t^3 - 18t^2 + 9t - 2)
g2 = grad(f1) x grad(g1) = 192(32st - 24s - 192t^4 + 576t^3 - 576t^2 + 190t - 7)
DF2 = [    4,    -6]
      [-1536, -6528]
```

Notice that the degree of `g0` is quadratic, `g1` is cubic and `g2` is quartic.

I think that the lightest weight change might simply to be to adjust the leeway to be something other than zero by default, and add it as an explicit param to PyJWT.decode, so that it's easy to find it with introspection tools or in the docstring for that function. As it is, I think that it's going to be pretty common for people to trip themselves up with the error if they don't know the external docs by heart.

When choosing between passing a value to leeway or disabling the check, I chose to disable the check. That might be because I'm coming more from a distributed database monkey's perspective, where I use timestamps to keep a record of things and help servers do things internally, but try to use other methods for syncing state across servers. I may be missing the security implications in skipping the check.

In any case, I think that I'd be happy with either solution -- just as long as the solution isn't to assume that all server clocks are perfectly in sync by default. It definitely makes sense to include it in a later version so that existing code doesn't break :-)

You can use local installation of Python 3.4 under your user account with [venv](https://docs.python.org/3/library/venv.html) (Python virtual environment - part of standard library in Python 3.3 and later).

Here is how:

```sh
# Download latest Python sources
curl -O https://www.python.org/ftp/python/3.4.3/Python-3.4.3.tgz

# Unpack sources archive
tar xf Python-3.4.3.tgz

# Install packages needed for Python build process
# (build-essential is maybe already preinstalled on Raspbian)
sudo apt-get install build-essential
sudo apt-get install python3-dev

# Build Python
cd Python-3.4.3
./configure
make

# Make independent python3.4 virtual environment in your home directory
mkdir ~/python
./python -m venv --copies ~/python/python3.4

# Activate python3.4 virtual environment
source ~/python/python3.4/bin/activate

# Upgrade setuptools and pip
pip install -U setuptools
pip install -U pip
```

Now everytime you want to use your local installation of Python instead of system-wide Python, you can run `source ~/python/python3.4/bin/activate` and install programs/modules with `pip` as you like (it will go into your local Python virtual environment).

```
def make_gensim_corpus(inpath,dictionaryfile,corpusfile):
    """Turn collection of text files into corpus for gensim."""
    ## Create list of document texts and list of document idnos.
    all_texts = []
    all_idnos = []
    for file in glob.glob(inpath): 
        with open(file) as infile:
            text = infile.read()
            text = re.split(" ", text)
            all_texts.append(text)
            idno,ext = os.path.splitext(os.path.basename(file))
            all_idnos.append(idno)
    ## Delete tokens which only appear once
    from collections import defaultdict
    frequency = defaultdict(int)
    for text in all_texts:
        for token in text:
            frequency[token] += 1
    all_texts = [[token for token in text if frequency[token] > 1] for text in all_texts]
    ## Build dictionary and corpus
    dictionary = corpora.Dictionary(all_texts)
    dictionary.compactify() # suggested by bmabey
    dictionary.save(dictionaryfile) # stores the dictionary
    dictionary.save_as_text("text_"+dictionaryfile) # stores the dictionary
    corpus = [dictionary.doc2bow(text) for item in all_texts]
    corpora.MmCorpus.serialize(corpusfile, corpus) # stores the corpus
    print("Done building the corpus.")
```

In the other case, aiosmtlib doesn't have any concept of keepalives or a connection pool or anything - it doesn't have a way to know that the server has disconnected until you try to send a command. `is_connected` just means the client *thinks* it has a connection. If you're entering the context manager each time, it should be disconnecting and reconnecting, but if you're not then all you can really do is handle the exception and retry. Something like:

```
try:
    await smtp_client.send_message(...)
except SMTPServerDisconnected:
    await smtp_client.connect()
    await smtp_client.login(...)
    await smtp_client.send_message(...)
```

If you just want to see if the connection is alive, you can see if `smtp_client.noop()` raises an error.

I think you still can do what was proposed to me in #11. The reason is that the close will not proceed until you call super().closeEvent(), and you can just do that from within the asynchronous function.

```python
# get asyncify from #11 
@asyncify
def closeEvent(self, ev):
    yield from some_long_task()
    super().closeEvent(ev)
```

From what I suspect, Qt implements the actual closing in the inherited closeEvent. If it does not work, you can still check out my proposed implementation of ``inline_async``. That should be legal (QDialog::exec is doing it, too), but not as nice as asyncify.

(In fact, it not only works for me with closeEvent, but also with most other synchronous slots and overriden methods. I have not seen any negative side effect. The reason seems to be that most interesting work is in fact done in the inherited methods. And you can just decide to call them from within the asynchronous task at a later point in time)

This is the block of code that contains line 124. It's still a work in progress. I just took the asa_upgrade code and added to it so you can handle insufficient space (delete files) and continue with the upgrade using a crude menu.

```
    # Check if file exists, check if there is enough space on the device, enable SCP, upload file, disable SCP
    with FileTransfer(ssh_conn, source_file=source_file, dest_file=dest_file,
                      file_system=dest_file_system) as scp_transfer:

        if not scp_transfer.check_file_exists():
            if not scp_transfer.verify_space_available():
                # Here we will handle insufficient space
                # We will loop and let user delete files until there is enough space
                while True:
                    output = ssh_conn.send_command("dir {0}".format(dest_file_system))
                    files = re.findall(regex_dir_w_d, output)

                    print(Fore.YELLOW + Style.BRIGHT + "Insufficient space on remote device. Choose file to delete:" + Style.RESET_ALL)

                    print("0   Don't delete any files")
                    for counter, file in enumerate(files):
                        size_MB = int(file[1]) / 1024000
                        file_name = file[2]
                        if file[0] == '-':
                            dir = ''
                        else:
                            dir = 'directory'
                        spaces = ' ' * (50 - len(file[2]) + 3)
                        print("{0}   {1}{2}{3}MB    {4}".format(counter + 1, file_name, spaces, round(size_MB, 2), dir))

                    while True:
                        option = int(input(Fore.CYAN + "Please choose an option: " + Style.RESET_ALL))
                        # Check if the option chosen is between 1 and length of files list + 1 (due to len coming up 1 short)
                        # print(list(range(1, len(files) + 1)))
                        if option in list(range(1, len(files) + 1)):
                            # Use recursive if file is a directory
                            if files[(option - 1)][0] == 'd':
                                cmd = "del /force /recursive {0}/{1}".format(dest_file_system, files[(option - 1)][2])
                            else:
                                cmd = "del /force {0}/{1}".format(dest_file_system, files[(option - 1)][2])
                            # Ask the user if they really want to delete the file
                            run_cmd = input(Fore.CYAN + "Run command: '{0}' (y/n): ".format(cmd) + Style.RESET_ALL)
                            if run_cmd == 'y':
                                output = ssh_conn.send_command(cmd)
                                print(output)
                                break
                            else:
                                # If user declines to run command to delete file then prompt for another file
                                pass
                        # If user enters 0 (don't delete any files) then raise error
                        elif option == 0:
                            raise ValueError(Fore.RED + Style.BRIGHT + "Insufficient space available on remote device" + Style.RESET_ALL)
                        else:
                            print(Fore.RED + Style.BRIGHT + "Please choose a valid option." + Style.RESET_ALL)

                    # We have gone through the menu loop and deleted a file, check space again
                    # If we have enough space the break out of loop, else check file system again and re-prompt user
                    if scp_transfer.verify_space_available():
                        break

            print(Fore.BLUE + Style.BRIGHT + "Enabling SCP" + Style.RESET_ALL)
            output = scp_handler(ssh_conn, mode='enable')
            print(output)

            print(Fore.BLUE + Style.BRIGHT + "Transferring file" + Style.RESET_ALL)
            scp_transfer.transfer_file()

            print(Fore.BLUE + Style.BRIGHT + "Disabling SCP" + Style.RESET_ALL)
            output = scp_handler(ssh_conn, mode='disable')
            print(output)

        # Verify the MD5 hash. If file already exists we will skip upload but still verify MD5.
        print(Fore.BLUE + Style.BRIGHT + "Verifying file" + Style.RESET_ALL)
        if scp_transfer.verify_file():
            print(Fore.GREEN + Style.BRIGHT + "Source and destination MD5 matches" + Style.RESET_ALL)
        else:
            raise ValueError("MD5 failure between source and destination files")
```

Scrolled a bit through the Python documentation, and it seems that `pip` always explicitly distinguishes between 'installing' and 'upgrading'. The example [here](https://docs.python.org/3/installing/index.html#basic-usage) mentions modules specifically, but it's the same mechanism, so I don't think this is any different.
> Normally, if a suitable module is already installed, attempting to install it again will have no effect. Upgrading existing modules must be requested explicitly: [..]

The pip-specific [documentation](https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/installing-packages/#upgrading-packages) doesn't mention it explicitly, but doesn't say otherwise either.

Python is a strongly typed language. That means it considers it an error
when you try to mix strings with bytes and errors out immediately when you
do so. It forces you to explicitly cast it to int and “be conscious” of
what you are doing. That is a good thing as it helps preventing subtle bugs
which you may discover later or never. Being psutil a python lib it makes
sense for it to follow the same (good) lead. Languages like php or
JavaScript are different in this regard.

@spulec First, thanks for this useful library. I don't agree though that pg8000 is at fault here, given there should be an implicit contract that freezegun will freeze time *without* changing object types with fake objects, otherwise this will cause unforeseen side effects (e.g. everytime some code check a `datetime`'s type). It's actually really surprising that psycopg2 doesn't complain!

I think it's actually possible to implement freezegun in this way, see the code below (only freezing `datetime.utwnow()` but can easily be generalized).

**fake_time.py**
```python
import datetime
import sys

real_datetime = datetime.datetime


def get_fake_datetime_class(frozen_datetime):
    class fake_datetime(datetime.datetime):
        def __new__(cls, *args, **kwargs):
            return real_datetime.__new__(real_datetime, *args, **kwargs)

        def utcnow(): return frozen_datetime
        # Also redefine all frozen datetime methods here to return frozen datetime objects
        # .
        # .
        # .

        def fromtimestamp(*args, **kwargs): return real_datetime.fromtimestamp(*args, **kwargs)
        # Also redefine all non-frozen datetime methods here to return datetime objects
        # .
        # .
        # .

    return fake_datetime


class freeze_time():
    def __init__(self, year, month, day, hour = 0, minute = 0, second = 0):
        self.frozen_datetime = real_datetime(year, month, day, hour, minute, second)

    def __enter__(self):
        # Memorize modified modules to clean state after use
        self.modified_modules = []

        for mod_name, module in list(sys.modules.items()):
            if (mod_name is None or module is None or mod_name == __name__
                    or mod_name.endswith('.six.moves')
                    or (not hasattr(module, "__name__"))
                    or module.__name__ in ('_datetime', 'datetime', 'time')):
                continue

            try:
                module_attributes = dir(module)
            except TypeError:
                module_attributes = []

            for attribute_name in module_attributes:
                try:
                    the_import = getattr(module, attribute_name)
                except (ImportError, AttributeError, TypeError):
                    continue
                else:
                    if (id(the_import) == id(real_datetime)):
                        self.modified_modules.append(module)
                        setattr(module, attribute_name, get_fake_datetime_class(self.frozen_datetime))

    def __exit__(self, exc_type, exc_value, traceback):
        for module in self.modified_modules:
            setattr(module, 'datetime', real_datetime)
```

**main.py**
```python
from fake_time import freeze_time
from datetime import datetime


print("No freeze")
print(datetime(2011,1,1))
print(type(datetime(2011,1,1)))
print(datetime.utcnow())
print(type(datetime.utcnow()))
print(datetime.fromtimestamp(213))
print(type(datetime.fromtimestamp(213)))


with freeze_time(2016, 5, 25, 1, 2, 33):
    print('')
    print("With freeze")
    print(datetime(2011,1,1))
    print(type(datetime(2011,1,1)))   # Constructor still returns a datetime object
    print(datetime.utcnow())
    print(type(datetime.utcnow()))    # utcnow() still returns a datetime object
    print(datetime.fromtimestamp(213))
    print(type(datetime.fromtimestamp(213)))   # Any method e.g. fromtimestamp still returns a datetime object
```

Hello,

the issue here is a little misunderstatement of what `invoke_without_command` truly does. Quoting the official documentation:



> **invoke_without_command**
>
> bool – Indicates if the group callback should begin parsing and invocation only if no subcommand was found. Useful for making it an error handling function to tell the user that no subcommand was found or to have different functionality in case no subcommand was found. If this is False, then the group callback will always be invoked first. This means that the checks and the parsing dictated by its parameters will be executed. Defaults to False.

In other words, using your code example, this means following outcomes:
1. `invoke_without_command=False`
```
< ??default hello
> default
> hello
```

2. `invoke_without_command=False` and no subcommand
```
< ??default
> default
```

3. `invoke_without_command=True`
```
< ??default hello
> hello
```

4. `invoke_without_command=True` and no subcommand
```
< ??default
> default
```

As you can see, the group name is required in all cases, the only thing that differs is if the group callback is called or not. I hope this makes it at least a little bit more clear.

Also, for future questions it is advised to use the official `discord.py` Discord server.

Hi Amber,

WhiteNoise is definitely intended to be used with a CDN, but Amazon S3 isn't a CDN -- it's a file storage service. Amazon's CDN service is called Cloudfront. Often Cloudfront and S3 are used together, but they don't have to be and this may be part of the confusion.

From those settings you've pasted I can see a couple of issues:
1. You define `STATICFILES_DIRS` in your DEBUG stanza, but don't define it for production
2. In production, your `STATIC_URL` setting starts with `https` but it has the value of `STATIC_HOST` interpolated into it which also starts `https` so the result won't be a valid URL.

I'll repeat my recommendation that the only thing that should differ between production and development (as far as static files go) is the value of `STATIC_URL`, as per the docs:
```python
STATIC_HOST = 'https://************.cloudfront.net' if not DEBUG else ''
STATIC_URL = STATIC_HOST + '/static/'
```
If you do that, and set up Cloudfront correctly, then everything should work and you'll be using a CDN in production. But of course you're free to set it how you want!

Best wishes,

Dave

Alright... I've been thinking about this for over a year now(!) You do warrant some reply by now :)

**I disagree with you!** :)

Some background: https://gist.github.com/XVilka/8346728

In brief: if ``number_of_colors`` is 256, we can pretty safely assume to support emitting 256 colors, and this is currently possible with ``term.color(196)`` or some such. However, as a "formatting name" it is deplorable. It would require using a reference guide to map colors-by-number.  I actually spent some time trying to find a mapping and came up pretty short, I guess you would run a script that emits all 256, pick the one you like, and note it down.

As an API, supporting ``{t.bold_color196}`` is very much against the "easy to write, easy to read" philosophy that Blessings encourages. What follows is my draft proposal.

rgb method
---------------
```
@property
def rgb(self)
   """
   Return a callable accepting arguments ``(red, green, blue)``, which emit a sequence approximated to the colorspace of the terminal bound by :attr:`number_of_colors`. The values of each color are 0-255.
(...)"""
```

This would be exactly like the existing ``color(n)``, except if given ``rgb(255, 50, 100)`` on a terminal where ``number_of_colors`` is 16, this would be "coerced" (or downsampled) as though you called ``rgb(255, 85, 85)`` which is exactly the expected color in the CGA palette, emitting the exact same sequence of ``term.bright_red`` or ``color(12)``.

true_colorspace context manager
---------------------------------------------

```
@contextmanager.contextlib
def true_colorspace():
   """
   Context manager that enables mapping of compound color names and :attr:`rgb` values
   to a True color (24-bit, 16,777,216 colors) colorspace.
   """
```

This is **necessary** as a context manager: we **cannot determine whether the given terminal emulator supports true colors**: There is no ``term-16777216color`` terminal name, or anything like it.  There is no situation that I know of where the terminal capability database for ``number_of_colors`` could return 16,777,216. A terminal emulator that supports it provides no method to query whether or not it supports it. For those who know their intended audience/emulator, they may rightly go ahead and use them, or provide configuration to enable it through the use of such context manager.

Compound Formatters as X11 color names
---------------------------------------------------------

Use the official X11 color names, http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/X11_color_names as compound formatters. These are mapped by their hexidecimal values to rgb() to emit the appropriate sequence. This allows one to use "Deep Pink", which is (255, 20, 147), but on a 16-color terminal it would be mapped to the same sequence as term.bright_red.

Try building your without using a sequential model. E.g. for VGG-16 do this;

```python
# get base model
base_model = applications.VGG16(weights='imagenet', include_top=False,input_shape = (img_width, img_height, 3))

# build top model
x = Flatten(name='flatten')(base_model.output)
x = Dense(4096, activation='relu', name='fc1')(x)
x = Dense(4096, activation='relu', name='fc2')(x)
x = Dense(3, activation='softmax', name='predictions')(x)

# stitch together
model = Model(inputs= base_model.input, outputs=x)

# inspect
model.summary()
```
This builds the model as one unified network rather than two cascading ones.

The z-order of shapes on a slide is determined solely by their document order in the slide part (e.g. slide1.xml). So the general gist would be to re-order that sequence of elements. The shapes in a slide are contained in the slide's "shape tree", a `<p:spTree>` element with the same syntax as the group shape, just the different name. The object I expect you'll want to look at first is pptx.shapes.shapetree.SlideShapeTree and its parent BaseShapeTree, which is what you get from `slide.shapes`. The `_spTree` attribute on that object gives you the lxml object for the `<p:spTree>` element, which would allow you to reorder shapes.

If you see how far you can get with that, feel free to ask more questions about the finer points, as needed :)

Hmm ... this is more complicated than I thought ...
I thought using `file.encode()` in a `try` block takes care of everything, but the problem is that in Python 2, the `str` type *also* has an `encode()` method (unlike `bytes` in Python 3).

Therefore, we'll either have to add an additional check for `not isinstance(file, bytes)` or replace the `try` block with a check for `unicode`, which seems to be awkward to do in a Python 2/3 compatible manner ...

Another problem is the call to `str()` when checking the file name extension. This raises an error in Python 2 if `file` (or `file.name`) is `unicode`.
Or what about an enhancement to this package that pings your server to see if it's a supported package, and if not, checks PyPi?

Here is a toy example:

```python
import opt_einsum as oe
from collections import defaultdict

# just a helper cache that tracks accesses
class CounterDict(dict):

    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.counts = defaultdict(lambda: 0)
        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)
        
    def __getitem__(self, k):
        self.counts[k] += 1
        return super().__getitem__(k)


# function that uses shared_intermediates (user may not even be aware it does so)
def my_useful_function(cache=None):
    X, Y, Z = oe.helpers.build_views('ab,bc,cd')
    
    if cache is None:
        cache = CounterDict()
    
    with oe.shared_intermediates(cache):
        # do some work
        import time
        time.sleep(0.5)
        
        Oa = oe.contract('ab,bc,cd->a', X, Y, Z)
        Ob = oe.contract('ab,bc,cd->b', X, Y, Z)
        
    return [(k, v) for k, v in cache.counts.items()]
```
What we expect everytime its run:
```python
my_useful_function()
# [(('einsum', 'numpy', 'ab,bc->a', (139971651561232, 139971651561312)), 1)]
```
i.e. that the cache is triggered once. Now multithread this:
```python
from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor

pool = ThreadPoolExecutor(4)
fs = [pool.submit(my_useful_function) for _ in range(4)]

# main thread might not want to cache here but...
print(len(oe.sharing._SHARING_STACK))
# 4
```
And then check the results:
```python
for f in fs:
    print(f.result())
# []
# []
# [(('einsum', 'numpy', 'ab,ca->c', (139971651178784, 139971651181424)), 1)]
# [(('einsum', 'numpy', 'ab,bc->a', (139971651180624, 139971651180944)), 1)]
```
So the cache is missed. If you try
```python
cache = CounterDict()
fs = [pool.submit(my_useful_function, cache) for _ in range(4)]
```
You will see that cache is hit many times however.

---

Yeah one answer yes would be just to make ``oe.sharing._SHARING_STACK`` itself a dict. With ``threading.get_ident()`` as keys, and a list of caches as the values (to enable nested sharing). I might give that a go in a sec.

It returns the positions by data ordered as inserted in the system (using a `collections.OrderedDict`). Because you are iterating over the dictionary, you get the keys (the datas).

The rationale behind is that you can iterate over the "items" and do something with the information given to you by the position understanting to which data the position is referring.

``` python
    def getpositions(self, broker=None):
        '''
        Returns the current by data positions directly from the broker

        If the given ``broker`` is None, the default broker will be used

        A property ``positions`` is also available
        '''
```

The same applies to `getpositionsbyname` in which you also get a `collections.OrderedDict`, but this time having the names as keys in the dictionary.

I can't see any case where this can be valid. In general, pyangbind will try to cast the input to the type that is expected, so for example, if we have the following module:

```yang
module "test" {
    prefix "t";
    namespace "http://rob.sh/yang/t";
    
    container test {
        leaf number {
            type uint32;
        }
        
        leaf number-range {
            type uint32 {
                range "1..max";
            }
        }
    }
}
```

Then, we can do:

```
>>> t.test.number = False
>>> t.test.number = 0
```

Since `int(False) == 0`, however:

```
>>> t.test.number = None
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "tmp.py", line 67, in _set_number
    raise ValueError("""number must be of a type compatible with base=np.uint32, is_leaf=True, yang_name="number", parent=self, path_helper=self._path_helper, extmethods=self._extmethods, register_paths=True""")
ValueError: number must be of a type compatible with base=np.uint32, is_leaf=True, yang_name="number", parent=self, path_helper=self._path_helper, extmethods=self._extmethods, register_paths=True
>>> int(None)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: int() argument must be a string or a number, not 'NoneType'
```

You cannot set an `int` to `None` because `NoneType` cannot be cast to `int`. So, even without the range, I don't think that this is valid. The reason that this is relevant is because the following JSON:

```json
{
    "value": null
}
```

is loaded by Python's `json` library to use None rather than null:

```
>>> import json
>>> j = json.load(open("test.json", 'r'))
>>> print j
{u'value': None}
```

In your specific case, this isn't ever valid though - because if `None`/`null` were an integer value then it would be zero - which is outside of the `1..max` range. With a normal leaf of this nature, one still get the error when one tries to set the value to 0:

```
>>> t.test.number_range = 0
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "tmp.py", line 93, in _set_number_range
    raise ValueError("""number_range must be of a type compatible with base=RestrictedClassType(base_type=np.uint32, restriction_dict={'range': [u'1..max']}), is_leaf=True, yang_name="number-range", parent=self, path_helper=self._path_helper, extmethods=self._extmethods, register_paths=True""")
ValueError: number_range must be of a type compatible with base=RestrictedClassType(base_type=np.uint32, restriction_dict={'range': [u'1..max']}), is_leaf=True, yang_name="number-range", parent=self, path_helper=self._path_helper, extmethods=self._extmethods, register_paths=True
>>> t.test.number_range = 1
```

So, I'd say that your input JSON is not valid.

> "...it would be even better if a documentation is made available." [Documentation](https://www.alkaline-ml.com/pyramid/) has been made available, and it's linked at the top of the repo: To save you the trouble, here is the documentation for [the ARIMA estimator class](https://www.alkaline-ml.com/pyramid/modules/generated/pyramid.arima.ARIMA.html#pyramid.arima.ARIMA), and here is the documentation for [the auto-ARIMA selection function](https://www.alkaline-ml.com/pyramid/modules/generated/pyramid.arima.auto_arima.html#pyramid.arima.auto_arima). And if you still want more, here is [the API ref](https://www.alkaline-ml.com/pyramid/modules/classes.html#api-ref), which links to all the documentation of each method. > "...how can we save the model that is returned by auto_arima, say to a file, then reuse it?" Pickling is handled the same way for classes here that it is for any other python object. Namely, `pickle.dump`: ```python from pyramid.arima import auto_arima from pyramid.datasets import load_lynx import numpy as np # For serialization: from sklearn.externals import joblib import pickle # Load data and fit a model y = load_lynx() arima = auto_arima(y, seasonal=True) # Serialize with Pickle, read it back and make a prediction with open('arima.pkl', 'wb') as pkl: pickle.dump(arima, pkl) with open('arima.pkl', 'rb') as pkl: pickle_preds = pickle.load(pkl).predict(n_periods=5) # Or maybe joblib tickles your fancy joblib.dump(arima, 'arima.pkl') joblib_preds = joblib.load('arima.pkl').predict(n_periods=5) # show they're the same np.allclose(pickle_preds, joblib_preds) ``` If your question is really more about serialization, you should see [this Stack Overflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/4530611/saving-and-loading-objects-and-using-pickle) question. This is a pretty standard serialization pattern for Python, which is why it wasn't explicitly documented. ### Final thoughts I'll concede to you that the library could use more examples, and that's something I can put together, but keep in mind, I'm one guy with a full-time job and I do this for nothing but my own amusement. I'm always happy to field issues as they come up, and I've integrated feature requests that have been requested by others in the past, but I've got to say... the way this issue was worded feels a little bit demanding. It's a bit silly to make demands of software you've gotten for free. That said, if you end up adding an example or documentation that you think would be valuable before I get a chance, please do submit a PR. Otherwise I'll get to it when I can.

```
from .logic_adapter import LogicAdapter
from chatterbot.conversation import Statement
from textblob.classifiers import NaiveBayesClassifier
from datetime import datetime


class CustomerSupportAdapter(LogicAdapter):
    """
    The TimeLogicAdapter returns the current time.
    """

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        super(CustomerSupportAdapter, self).__init__(**kwargs)
        
        
        
        training_data = [
            ("Are international students elibgible", 1),
            ("Im an international student, can I apply", 1),
            ("whats available to international students", 1),
            ("international students", 1),
            ("do international students qualify", 1),
            ("international students accepted", 1),
            ("do i qualify as an international student", 1),
            ("as an international student do i qualify", 1),
            ("what are the requirements for international students", 1)
        ]

        self.classifier = NaiveBayesClassifier(training_data)

    def process(self, statement):
        # now = datetime.now()
        confidence = self.classifier.classify(statement.text.lower())
        response = Statement("The Mount Baker, Washington program is open to international students")

        return confidence, response
```

No worries.. I've been poking at this today, I'm going to land a version of it that fixes a few more things. I don't entirely agree with pep8, but I'm ok with targetting it for uniformity.

`autopep8` doesn't seem to fix everything that `flake8` complains about: I think I've been spoiled with how well `rustfmt` works over in Rust land.

And flake8 presents some impossible situations like:

```python
if ("foo" in stuff and
    "bar" not in stuff):
    do_things()
```

(where the conditional is too big to fit on a single line)

flake8 complains that the `do_things()` visually lines up with the inside of the conditional statement, which is true, but it also doesn't accept some extra whitespace in the second half of the condition since then the second half won't line up with the first half. The only way I've found to appease it is to use *two* parentheses, which just seems silly.

Also for files like `test_ipaddrs.py`, I'd like to retain the original (overlength) lines of sample output from `ifconfig` rather than wrap them with `\` to address the long-line complaint. But there's no way to disable the long-ine complaint for just a single line inside a literal block-quoted string, nor is there a way to disable just one warning on a per-file basis. So I've left the line-wrapping in place for now.


Anyways, I'm going to land this, and see how it feels going forward.

You just use the "poly" method in the Writer.  The poly method isn't just for polygons, it can do polylines and polypoints!  

Here's a simple Multipoint shapefile:

```import shapefile

# Create our writer as a multipoint shapefile
w = shapefile.Writer(shapefile.MULTIPOINT)

# We'll create a single dbf field
w.field("NAME","C",40)

# Create a single-part, multi-point shape by declaring the shape
# type after the parts/points list
w.poly([[[3,4],[5,6],[7,8],[9,8]]], shapeType=shapefile.MULTIPOINT)

# Create a record for this feature
w.record("Group1")

# Save the multipoint shapefile
w.save("mpoint")
```

```py
from time import sleep
```

> When you want to revert to an older revision, you get a regular change

> version you are reverting to, though the log message still only says
> "Reverted to previous version".
>
> This commit changes the reverting code to create a separate revision for
> the revert, with the "Reverted to previous version" message. The
> subsequent save of the form happens in a second revision.
>
> *This change is not complete*: Currently the second revision is always
> saved, regardless of whether any changes were made. I am not sure how to
> solve this yet.
>
> I tried setting ignore_duplicates on the relevant model, which seems
> to work. Of course this is something that should not be set for the
> entire model, but just for this particular revision, but that is
> probably feasible to add. However, ignore_duplicates seems to work on
> individual versions, so might cause some versions to be left out of the
> revision, even when the revision is not completely empty (more
> generally, I believe ignore_duplicates might not always work as
> intended when multiple models or follows are involved).
>
> One other approach might be to to another check (like the one for
> ignore_duplicates) just before saving a revision, but check *all*
> versions in the revision and skip saving if *all* versions are

> comparing these versions is probably acceptable when it only happens on

I think you can accomplish want you want already because you can pivot on an annotated column.

```
from django.db.models import Case, When, CharField

annotation = {
    'pivot_column': Case(When(Q(stage='negotiating', is_archived=False), then=Value('Negotiating')),
                         When(Q(...), then=Value('Closed'),
                         When(Q(...), then=Value('Archived'),
                         default=None,
                         output_field=CharField()
}

queryset = Lead.objects.annotate(**annotation)
pivot_table = pivot(queryset, 'sales_rep', 'pivot_column', 'id', aggregation=Count)
```

If there is a cleaner API than the above, maybe it's worth having the pivot package expose that. I'm certainly open to making pivoting on an annotated column cleaner, but I'm not thinking of something better off the top of my head. The `cases` list in your example isn't that different than what's in the annotation.

```python
# Load a sample picture and learn how to recognize it.
```

What happened here is that `face_encodings` loaded an image and find faces(note that its plural, not `face` but `faces`) in that image. Once thats done, it translated those faces into numpy array of 128 features.

So what you have in the end is a 2D array with first dimension being the faces and the 2nd dimension being the facial encoding features (size 128)
`camera.capture(output, format="rgb")` writes the output into the `output` variable. It doesn't return anything from the function call. So the frame data will be in `output`.

A better fix may be to implement sending `class_args` to `injector.create_object` ([on line 115](https://github.com/alecthomas/flask_injector/blob/master/flask_injector.py#L115)), but this is easier and only affects one repo rather than two. However, not adding `class_args` on the object may have repercussions further down the line.

My solution going @mbi suggestions and using memory caching. It works only for last step.

```python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-
from django.core.exceptions import ValidationError
from django.utils.translation import ugettext_lazy

from captcha.fields import CaptchaField
from captcha.conf import settings
from captcha.models import CaptchaStore, get_safe_now


class CaptchaWizardField(CaptchaField):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        self.validity_count = kwargs.pop('validity_count', 2)
        self.validity_cache = {}
        super(CaptchaWizardField, self).__init__(*args, **kwargs)

    def clean(self, value):
        super(CaptchaField, self).clean(value)
        CaptchaStore.remove_expired()

        response, value[1] = (value[1] or '').strip().lower(), ''
        hashkey = value[0]

        if settings.CAPTCHA_TEST_MODE and response.lower() == 'passed':
            # automatically pass the test
            try:
                # try to delete the captcha based on its hash
                CaptchaStore.objects.get(hashkey=hashkey).delete()
            except CaptchaStore.DoesNotExist:
                # ignore errors
                pass
        elif not self.required and not response:
            pass
        else:
            # let enable validity_count times
            # of clean() method
            if hashkey in self.validity_cache and self.validity_cache[hashkey] > 0:
                self.validity_cache[hashkey] -= 1
                return value
            try:
                captcha = CaptchaStore.objects.get(response=response, hashkey=hashkey, expiration__gt=get_safe_now())
                self.validity_cache[hashkey] = self.validity_count - 1
                captcha.delete()
            except CaptchaStore.DoesNotExist:
                raise ValidationError(getattr(self, 'error_messages', {}).get('invalid',
                                                                              ugettext_lazy('Invalid CAPTCHA')))
        return value
```

And then:

```python
class WizardFormLast(forms.Form):
    captcha = CaptchaWizardField()
```

Yes, you should tag a release and upload to GitHub the source distribution and the compiled wheels.

To make the .tar.gz and .zip source distributions:
```bash
python setup.py sdist --formats=gztar,zip
```

to make the compiled wheels (built for the python version and platform of the python interpreter used to produce them):
```
# better updating these first, just in case
pip install --upgrade wheel setuptools pip
# then from the root of booleanOperations git repo
```

You'll find both the compiled wheel and the source distributions in the `dist/` subfolder.

With non-secured sockets, it works as usual, reading only reading_buffer_size at a time.

With secured sockets, even if I ask to read reading_buffer_size, the SSL object may decide to read more from the underlying socket. It keeps its own buffer. The issue is that, the poll loop will not trigger any read events and the buffer will not be made visible to the websocket interface. This is why you had to send an empty message before my fix. This would trigger the poller again and the websocket interface would finally read content from the SSL socket object (unaware it was reading its buffer actually).

What my fix does (based on that SO's article) is that I explicitly read the buffer at once. So, indeed we actually end up with more than reading_buffer_size in the bytes sent down to the parser.

This means, in effect, that the gradual reading from the socket mechanism the websocket interface uses, is no longer respected in the case of secure sockets (except for very large sockets that go beyond the size of a SSL frame).

Interestingly, this might mean that ws4py has better performances with SSL activated.

I have a function to use word files as snipplets:
`def __ext_snipplet(self, name):
    """
    Render another docx body into my document
    """
    document = DocxTemplate(self.getSnippletPath(name))
    document.render(self.jinjaContext)
    # Remove the sectPr element ("SecrionProperties")
    # "This element defines the section properties for the final section of the document." (Source: https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/documentformat.openxml.wordprocessing.sectionproperties(v=office.14).aspx)
    # This is not needed in case of a snipplet
    if document._element.body.sectPr is not None:
        document._element.body.remove(document._element.body.sectPr)
    # Remove the body xml tag
    xml = re.sub(r'</?w:body[^>]*>', '', document.get_xml())
    return xml`

You have to add it to the jinja context:
`self.jinjaContext.update({'snipplet': self.__ext_snipplet})`

Then, in your template you can call other word files. They will be rendered too:

Work-around is as follows; put the overriding logic into your app's `view.py` file:

```
from django.core.urlresolvers import reverse
from django.core import signing
from password_reset.views import Recover

class RecoverView(Recover):
    def form_invalid(self, form):
        # We want to make believe that an invalid user/email is actually valid
        user = form.data['username_or_email']
        self.mail_signature = signing.dumps(user, salt=self.url_salt)
        return super(Recover, self).form_valid(form)

    def form_valid(self, form):
        # Overridden so as to not allow for enumeration of email via username
        self.user = form.cleaned_data['user']
        username_or_email = form.data['username_or_email']
        self.send_notification()
        self.mail_signature = signing.dumps(username_or_email,
                                            salt=self.url_salt)
        return super(Recover, self).form_valid(form)
```
Both, `form_valid` and `form_invalid` have to be overridden, because there is a second enumeration vulnerability in the `form_valid` function, where upon successful entry of a username that exists in the system will display a message stating that an email was sent to the the entered username's email address.  By displaying the email address here, anyone can find a valid user and their email address by attempting password resets with usernames.

The changes made will make it so that the value entered on the reset form is the same value that generates the end URL and is the same value that gets displayed on the results page, and this is what will get displayed whether the username or email is valid or not.

If you would like a pull request with these changes, just say the word.

 - One should be allowed to create intervals with "reversed bounds" (ie. lower is greater than upper). Currently, this is prevented for no specific reason given that such intervals can be considered as a way to represent empty intervals. 
 - If we allow "reversed bounds", then we could represent empty intervals as (I.inf, -I.inf). This way, all operations involving an empty interval will be consistent (because everything is greater than -I.inf and lower than I.inf, meaning that empty intervals will be considered as contained in any other ones, as greater/lower than any other ones, and as having no overlap with any other ones).

According to : 
https://github.com/indygreg/python-zstandard#comparison-to-other-python-bindings

it seems this python wrapper generates its own compressed format, thanks to a custom header.
The claim looks supported by :
https://github.com/sergey-dryabzhinsky/python-zstd/blob/master/src/python-zstd.c#L88


Note that there is nothing wrong with creating one's own format.
Just, a downside is that files created with a custom header won't be decodable by 3rd party decompressor expecting to read [zstd frame format](https://github.com/facebook/zstd/blob/dev/zstd_compression_format.md).

Reason I'm asking is that, similar to [lz4](http://cyan4973.github.io/lz4/#interoperable-lz4), I wish to list bindings within 2 categories, interoperable and custom format, and I need to list your binding in the correct category.

Yes, I was looking into the metrics, and I didn't feel like having a separate perplexity metric was necessary in gluon. The reason is that perplexity is usually just the exp(cross_entropy_loss). Since cross_entropy_loss is required for training and often used in testing, getting perplexity is just about calculating its exponential. Having a separate perplexity in that case could mean unnecessarily repeated computation.

@sbodenstein I agree. There is only one catch. It seems that the current WarpCTC supports variable-length inputs whereas cudnn7 only has the intention to support it. To elaborate, the current cudnn7 CTC API for getting workspace size looks like this:
```
cudnnStatus_t CUDNNWINAPI cudnnGetCTCLossWorkspaceSize(
                                cudnnHandle_t                       handle,
                                const cudnnTensorDescriptor_t       probsDesc,       /* Tensor descriptor for probabilities, the dimensions are T,N,A (T is the timing steps, N is the mini batch size, A is the alphabet size) */
                                const cudnnTensorDescriptor_t       gradientsDesc,   /* Tensor descriptor for gradients, the dimensions are T,N,A. To compute costs only, set it to NULL */
                                const int                          * labels,         /* labels, in CPU memory */
                                const int                          * labelLengths,   /* the length of each label, in CPU memory */
                                const int                          * inputLengths,   /* the lengths of timing steps in each batch, in CPU memory */
                                cudnnCTCLossAlgo_t                  algo,            /* algorithm selected, supported now 0 and 1 */
                                cudnnCTCLossDescriptor_t            ctcLossDesc,
                                size_t                             *sizeInBytes );   /* pointer to the returned workspace size */
```
However, if I give any `*inputLengths` that is different from T, it throws an `CUDNN_STATUS_BAD_PARAM` error. Reading the doc, it has a note saying that this error is thrown if:
```
‣ The inputLengths do not agree with the first dimension of probsDesc.
```
...so my guess is that this argument is left there so that variable input lengths will be supported going forward, because asking for a list of Ts doesn't make much sense otherwise.

Back to whether we should remove the WarpCTC implementation, I think we need to first clarify with cudnn team on when the `*inputLengths` can support variable input lengths, and remove the WarpCTC GPU implementation when that becomes available.

Played around with this for a bit because I was confused by @dannyroberts example above. The two dates in that example are NOT the same—they're off by almost 10 seconds. I was confused by that and initially thought he may have been advocating for some kind of fuzzy matching that discards small time differences, but the same argument could be made for float values so that didn't make sense. Anyway, here's some code:
```py
>>> import datetime
>>> import pytz
>>> now = datetime.datetime.now(pytz.timezone('UTC'))
>>> now
datetime.datetime(2018, 3, 30, 11, 48, 15, 255346, tzinfo=<UTC>)
>>> az_now = now.astimezone(pytz.timezone('US/Arizona'))
>>> pc_now = now.astimezone(pytz.timezone('US/Pacific'))
>>> az_now
datetime.datetime(2018, 3, 30, 4, 48, 15, 255346, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'US/Arizona' MST-1 day, 17:00:00 STD>)
>>> pc_now
datetime.datetime(2018, 3, 30, 4, 48, 15, 255346, tzinfo=<DstTzInfo 'US/Pacific' PDT-1 day, 17:00:00 DST>)
>>> az_now == pc_now
True
>>> az_now.isoformat() == pc_now.isoformat()
True
>>> repr(az_now) == repr(pc_now)
False
```
One trivial way to make them differ would be to use `repr` before comparing if you need datetimes with different timezones to break the cache. However, I don't think that's a good idea. Since `az_now == pc_now` returns `True`, it seems reasonable to have quickcache consider these two dates as equal. This seems intuitive to me, especially given how it coerces bytes and unicode to mean the same thing even when they are not equal.

Alternately, maybe it would make sense to add a feature where custom serialization functions could be registered with quickcache so this behavior can be customized?
That's probably not intended, but mixing zmq.Context and zmq.asyncio.Context is likely to get something into trouble.

There is definitely a bug. There are two bugs in your scripts, though: the send calls need to be awaited as well. Make sure to `await dealer.send` and `await router.send_multipart`. This doesn't fix the issue entirely, but it does improve the situation.

The bug is gone if you use `await send(router, identity, msg)` instead of `ansyncio.ensure_future(send(...)` in `server.py`. This suggests that the bug occurs when both an async send and recv are pending on the same socket, e.g. something along the lines of

```
start waiting for recv
start waiting for send
send happens
recv never wakes
```

`MSVCP140.DLL` and `VCRUNTIME140.DLL` are not the wrong CRT DLLs to use for extensions compiled with Visual Studio 2015 for CPython 3.5. It looks like the "fixed" pyzmq binaries were built with `DISTUTILS_USE_SDK` set, which statically links the CRT. See the [blog of Steve Dower](http://stevedower.id.au/blog/building-for-python-3-5-part-two/) why this is not a good idea. Note that the pyzmq binaries for CPython 2.7 and 3.4 also depend on compiler specific CRT DLLs, `MSVCR*.DLL` and `MSVCP*.DLL`.

One of the reasons to look at training and validation WER while training is to look for overfitting. With a skewed training set the training WER is not comparable with the validation WER and thus overfitting can not be measured while training.

As an example, say we have 1 billion training examples we weight example 0 with weight 1 and all others with weight 0. Our model learns perfectly example 0 and our training WER is 0.0. We run the same model over our validation set and unsurprisingly get WER 1.0.

Naively, looking at only the WER's, one would think the model is overfitting the training set as the training WER is 0.0. However, because of the weighting, this is false. It's overfitting only a small subset of the training set.

Thus, as this simply example illustrates, the WER for a weighted training set can't be used for checking overfitting.

To get a meaningful result one has to calculate the training WER using a unweighted training set.

@seanjensengrey - OK final version for datetime conversion, if you have a sec to test in your env:

```python
import datetime

epoch = datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(0)


def unix_time_millis(dt):
    td = dt - epoch
    tdms = ((td.days * 24 * 3600) + td.seconds) * 1000
    ms = td.microseconds // 1000
    return tdms + ms


def datetime_from_unix_time_millis(ut):
    if isinstance(ut, float):
        raise ValueError('unix timestamp must not be a float, \
                it must be total milliseconds since epoch as an integer')
    utms = ut / 1000.0
    return datetime.datetime.utcfromtimestamp(utms)


dt_1001 = datetime_from_unix_time_millis(1001)
print('dt_1001:', dt_1001)
utm_1001 = unix_time_millis(dt_1001)
print('utm_1001:', utm_1001)
```

Yep, the `unquote(urlparse(uri))` constructs result in `/C:/Apps/...` path instead of `C:\Apps\..` which causes jedi to add another `C:\` to the path as it interprets the leading slash as relative path.

I was able to fix the issue by using the following function from LSP package as replacement for `unquote(urlparse(uri))` in workspace.py

```
def uri_to_filename(uri: str) -> str:
    if os.name == 'nt':
        path = url2pathname(uri.replace("file://", ""))
    else:
        path = url2pathname(uri).replace("file://", "")
    # debug(uri, path)
    return path
```

@hellupline  JSONAPI specs require the content type to be set as "Content-Type: application/vnd.api+json" and not Content-Type: application/json.  (http://jsonapi.org/format/ )
Same goes for GET requests to API endpoints, GET requests should be made be Accept header set as application/vnd.api+json. 

Anyways, Content-Type: application/vnd.api+json is still there in flask-rest-jsonapi for POST and PATCH requests; But, Accept header is missing in GET. It simply does not return any error if Accept header is not set.  

Although, above behavior with Accept header is only on this repo's master branch (v0.13.2). Luckily, develop branch (v0.12.5) supports JSONAPI specs strictly. So, using that one now. 

We just like following all specs a bit seriously, that's it. So, was the reason for moving to flask-rest-jsonapi; Flask-Restless was just placeholder to get our front end guys get started on app. We were anyways planning to move to Flask-Restful for its flexibility, flask blueprint support and so much more. Only their request parser wasn't jsonapi compliant. Hence, I was experimenting around Restful to integrate it with marshmallow_jsonapi; then I came across flask-rest-jsonapi.  It was only recently, when we were freezing restless to staging and started seriously on flask-rest-jsonapi (using its master), I noticed it allows GET requests with missing headers too. Hope this gets resolved soon. And, how come develop has lower version number and supports a desired feature, which is missing in a later version? @akira-dev

I would advise to hard code `'us-east-1'` when you create the client:
```python
client = boto3.client('cur', 'us-east-1')
```
The `cur` service is only available in `us-east-1` so when you specify `eu-west-1` in the config file, boto3 will try to connect to that endpoint but it does not exist so it will fail to connect.

If you've received a response from the service (but the instances aren't yet in the `running` state, you'll get a reservation id back that you can use in `describe_instances` to get a list of all instances in that reservation.

If you mean the call is interrupted *before* you receive a response, your best option is to use the `ClientToken` parameter.  You generate some random token and pass it through in your call to `create_instances`.  You can then to a `describe_instances` call and filter based on that token you generated to get a list of instances associated with that token.  From there you can stop/terminate those instances.

@russellballestrini 

> I have found myself writing lots of helpers or wrappers to make the library appear more native to Python.

Same here. I think there is room in the world for a `boto3util` library or something that makes common operations like tag lookup more Pythonic. Or perhaps the boto3 maintainers will consider adding something like that directly to the main library.

boto:

```python
tag_value = instance.tags['tag_key']
```

boto3:

```python
# Method 1
tag_value = [tag['Value'] for tag in instance.tags if tag['Key'] == 'tag_key'][0]

# Method 2
for tag in instance.tags:
    if tag['Key'] == 'tag_key':
        tag_value = tag['Value']
        break
else:
    raise KeyError(...)
```

In the case of tag lookup, the original boto definitely beats boto3 in terms of being idiomatic Python. A given instance's tags represent a set of keys and values, and the keys must be unique. This is pretty much what a dictionary is, and accessing tags as anything other than a single dictionary seems off. 

Unfortunately, changing boto3 to follow this behavior at this point would be a backwards-incompatible change, so it will probably never happen. The most practical way out is probably through separate utility methods that the user has to call.

In this case you're being throttled because you're sending requests rapid-fire one after another. For a more robust waiting solution, you should use a [waiter](http://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/rds.html#RDS.Waiter.DBInstanceAvailable):

```python
def get_endpoint_status(instance_name):
        conn = boto3.client('rds')
        return conn.describe_db_instances(DBInstanceIdentifier=instance_name)['DBInstances'][0]['DBInstanceStatus']

def get_endpoint(brand_name=BRAND_NAME):
        instance_name = "rde-db-rds-{}".format(brand_name)
        current_status = None
        conn = boto3.client('rds')
        # This will return once the instance is available
        conn.get_waiter('db_instance_available').wait(DBInstanceIdentifier=instance_name)
        response = conn.describe_db_instances(DBInstanceIdentifier=instance_name)
        return response['DBInstances'][0]['Endpoint']['Address']
```

Memory use is way up because intermediate bitmaps are cached in the new algorithm. backrefs.py is dead, replaced by graph.py. It works like this:

1. Each blob includes itself, so give it a bitmap with only itself marked.
2. Do a topological sort, starting from each of the blobs you want to look up.
3. For each vertex in the sorted list, OR its bitmap into the child's bitmap. If the child doesn't have a bitmap, give it a copy of the current vertex bitmap. If the child is a commit, OR the vertex bitmap into the result set/dict instead.
4. Delete the current vertex bitmap.

Another side effect of this algorithm is you can partition the search. You could in theory do it with a step size of 1 and that would be the same as the old way. Or you can split it in half. This reduces the memory required. The code handles this, and theoretically the partitions can be done concurrently if you adapt the way temporary bitmaps are generated.

In order to do it all in one go it is necessary to reduce the graph. This is the part I am not sure is correct. Without reduction I have 350 million edges, and generating the bitmaps all at once would need 32GB of RAM. With reduction it fits in about 8GB.

I have tested against latest develop. I don't have time right now to reproduce it with a small project but I'll gladly share my current findings, hoping they will be of any use.

I created two different pages attached to two different Sites, and set each of them to a different AppConfig. Because of this, the following code (from `djangocms_blog/cms_wizards.py`) goes two times in the loop (because I have two `BlogConfig` objects):

```python
for config in BlogConfig.objects.all().order_by('namespace'):                         
    new_wizard = type(str(slugify(config.app_title)), (PostWizard,), {})
    new_form = type(str('{0}Form').format(slugify(config.app_title)), (PostWizardForm,    ), {    
        'default_appconfig': config.pk
    })  
    post_wizard = new_wizard(
        title=_('New {0}').format(config.object_name),
        weight=200,
        form=new_form,
        model=Post,
        description=_('Create a new {0} in {1}').format(config.object_name, config.app    _title),    
    )
    try:
        wizard_pool.register(post_wizard)
    except AlreadyRegisteredException:  # pragma: no cover
        if settings.DEBUG:
            raise
```

When running `wizard_pool.register`, the following code is executed (from `cms/wizards/wizard_pool.py`):
```python
 def register(self, entry):
    """
    Registers the provided «entry».

    Raises AlreadyRegisteredException if the entry is already registered.
    """
    assert isinstance(entry, Wizard), u"entry must be an instance of Wizard"
    if self.is_registered(entry, passive=True):
        model = entry.get_model()
        raise AlreadyRegisteredException(
            _(u"A wizard has already been registered for model: %s") %
            model.__name__)
```

The `is_registered` method checks if the `id` attribute of the entry is already present in the registered entries. It will run fine for the 1st wizard but will raise an exception for the second wizard because the `id` property is computed from the `self.__module__` and `self.__class__.__name__` attributes, which are the same for the two wizards created previously.

I hope that's clear and that my analysis is correct. I have absolutely no idea how to solve it though. ;) Maybe by never raising an exception if a wizard is already registered and just ignore it?

This has been discussed multiple times in the past. You can search the issue tracker for similar entries. To sum things, it is not possible (at this time; can’t say for the future) to specify the name of the virtualenv, since Pipenv relies on this information to associate it to your project. The alternative is to set environment variable `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` to tell Pipenv to look for the a directory named `.venv` inside your project (the same level as `Pipfile`).

To import dependencies from `requirements.txt`, use `pipenv install -r`. This converts the requirements file to a Pipfile, and use it to create the environment. There is not way to specify an existing virtualenv (unless you name it exactly the same as Pipenv would).

Most of the above is mentioned in detail in the documentation. Please check out relevant sections for more information.

https://docs.pipenv.org

Hey @vstoykov, thanks for reaching out. Could I ask why you're using this notation and why you expect it to work? This isn't documented as working for Pip in the official documentation as far as I can tell, and conceptually doesn't really make sense. The examples using the fragment in the documentation for [`pip install`](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/reference/pip_install/#vcs-support) show that a package name with a version should be represented as <project_name>-<version>. There's also the semantics of key value pairs represented as `k=v` and this notation breaks parsing for that.

The fact that this works is because pip is currently ignoring the egg fragment since it's installing straight from the source repository. I don't have an issue patching this so we exit in a nicer fashion, but I'm not convinced this is something we should be supporting. If I'm missing something though, please let us know and I'm happy to change my mind if we have a compelling reason.
