Yeah, I've seen that before. The problem is that some frameworks use different encodings for the public key types. 

(Probably more info than you want: The ECDSA public key is a pair of 32byte octets, thus the 64 bytes. It's prefixed by a "RAW" key flag (the `\x04`) Some frameworks use a different key storage mechanism that encodes the key into a totally different format that's a pain in the butt to extract, unless you run it though a ASN1 library. Basically, if your key isn't 64 bytes, or 65 with the first byte being `\x04`, it's probably ASN1 and needs to be transcoded. Sadly: not super happy fun times, but possible. I'll try to add some notes tomorrow if that makes things easier for you.)

The spec requires that the public key be in RAW DER format (`\x04' + 2*32 bytes).

More fun news! The VAPID format is changing soon. Draft02 moves stuff as a prefix for the AUTH key. 

No, really, feel free to use a library to deal with this nonsense. 

You have a life. You should spend more time enjoying it.

(Well, unless you find this sort of sadness fun, in which case, feel free to pitch in over on the VAPID libs)

It resembles cargo container. Say,

```
tasks: walltime='00:10:00', mem='10G', cores=1
```
and the container has

```
container_mem: 32G
container_walltime: '10:00:00'
container_cores: 8
```
Then the task engine will put certain amount of tasks into one job, executing 3 tasks at a time (< 32G), and up to 180 tasks (done in 10 hours) in total. The PBS system will only see these big containers, not individual tasks, but users will see the status of each tasks.

```
input: concurrent=True
statement 1

it's certainly not ideal to put this in the driver, but it was the fastest
option I could think of at the time of writing the code.

At the moment the way logging is handled is just the default python output
errors to the command prompt - so by default it only prints warnings and
anything more critical than that. (we don't yet have a dedicated logging

wanted the log.info level set for this magnet driver only, without
disturbing the rest of the workflow (the magnet driver will probably get
called at different places in the code by different routines, depending on
who is using it).

source - or perhaps in general how to handle/approach this particular case,
please let me know.

experiment scripts, which can get messy quite quickly.

Tiny notes from looking into: 
https://github.com/npm/read-package-json/blob/latest/read-json.js which calls:
https://github.com/npm/normalize-package-data/blob/master/lib/normalize.js which calls into:
https://github.com/npm/normalize-package-data/blob/master/lib/fixer.js

Effectively, as far as i can tell, there are some fallback paths here so that people can write stuff like:

`dependencies: "..."`

i.e. a string, instead of an object.  In that case, the string will be split on whitespace/command to get a list of strings:

https://github.com/npm/normalize-package-data/blob/df8ea05b8cd38531e8b70ac7906f420344f55bab/lib/fixer.js#L384 

`dependencies: [...]` is also supported (and is waht the above string codepath calls into).

There an array of strings is allowed.  Those string are then split, and the pairs of each string become a key/value dependency here:

https://github.com/npm/normalize-package-data/blob/df8ea05b8cd38531e8b70ac7906f420344f55bab/lib/fixer.js#L392-L397

---

Now: if we ignore htat funky logic, hte only other special thing this library does is to call addOptionalDepsToDeps: 

https://github.com/npm/normalize-package-data/blob/df8ea05b8cd38531e8b70ac7906f420344f55bab/lib/fixer.js#L371-L379

This then allows for a `optionalDependencies: { ... }` section which is merged into the normal `dependencies` section.

---

This is the only special logic that happens.  Otherwise, we can effectively just json parse the package.json file, then extract the .dependencies section, and just read its property names.

Thanks! This isn't a detailed comparison, but here are some thoughts that come to mind. And I agree there are lots of opportunities for collaboration/inspiration!

- **Remote function API:** The `ray.remote` decorator is similar to `dask.delayed`, but I think it is more similar to Dask's `client.submit` API.

- **Actors:** An important part of the Ray API is the [actor abstraction](http://ray.readthedocs.io/en/latest/actors.html) for sharing mutable state between tasks (e.g., the state of a neural network or the state of a simulator). This blends very nicely with the side-effect free dataflow abstraction and is important for our workloads both to share state and to avoid expensive initializations. I don't think there is an analogue in Dask.

- **Collections:** Dask has extensive high-level collections APIs (e.g., dataframes, distributed arrays, etc), whereas Ray does not.

- **Scheduling:** Ray uses a distributed bottom-up scheduling scheme in which workers submit tasks to local schedulers, and local schedulers assign tasks to workers. Local schedulers can also forward tasks to global schedulers which can load balance between machines. Dask uses a [centralized scheduler](https://distributed.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#architecture), which manages all tasks for the cluster. The point of the bottom-up scheduling approach is to improve task latency and throughput.

- **Data:** Ray stores objects in an object store and serializes them using Apache Arrow (there is one object store process per machine). Worker processes can access objects in the object store through shared memory with minimal deserialization (and without copying the data). I don't think Dask has an analogue of the Ray object store.

- **System state/metadata:** Ray uses a sharded database (implemented as multiple Redis servers), to store the metadata and control state of the system. This is a pretty central feature of our current design, and leads to lots of different design decisions.

And presumably lots of other things as well. @mrocklin please correct me if I said something wrong.

There's this one weird thing where num_envs_per_worker will reduce your
effective unroll length per env (so 20 / 5 = unroll length of 4). So just
watch out for that and you might consider trying 1 env per worker instead,
or setting sample_batch_size=50 for a longer unroll.

Beyond that the config looks fine. Note that I found a lr schedule is
important for some envs (but it's probably too much to try right now).

Asyncio does its best but it's not a terribly reliable source for this kind of thing. On Windows there's the equivalent of `waitpid`, but it's straightforwardly cancellable, so you can just call it in a thread and then give up if you want to.

> At minimum we need a WeakValueDictionary of pid>waiter records, because we can't cancel the waiting thread

Oh, I see, because you want to wait on an arbitrary pid that some other code spawned. If someone starts a process by calling `trio.subprocess.Process(...)`, then the `Process` object itself can hold the state that the thread uses to coordinate.

But I guess you are thinking in terms of trio-asyncio using some hybrid of asyncio's process handling code and trio's process handling code? I suspect that's not going to work satisfactorily, and we'll eventually want to implement the process protocol handling stuff on top of `trio.subprocess` so that it handles things in a uniform trio way. Hi, there are nothing stopping us from going ahead and deal with NCHW using a global flag in fact a patch is actively being worked on that does it. 
However, we are also interested in pushing a minimally intrusive change to the Spec for the most natural/error-proof way of dealing with this issue. Let me walk you through three ideas we had and benefit/drawbacks:
1. I assume your "Graph Rewriter" is some sort of graph optimizer to eliminate transpose. Seems to be a lot of work involved in implementing the infrastructure for such an optimizer and may end up rebuilding a good portion of the wheel ONNX generic optimizer has already built.
2. Global flag to specify default data format. This is our current approach but I fear that this involves a lot of un-checkable assumptions to be made. I.e, my predecessor node has given me the tensor in the correct format. Such assumption, if broken, is terrible to debug/trace. In addition, we also have filters in different data format. The list will grow...
3. Operation-level data-format flag: are we really going to change the input signature of a whole bunch of critical ops like (Conv, Pool) and not support it in the majority of the frameworks? Doesn't sound appealing to other backend developers.
4. Named dimensions. As discussed above, the assumptions made in this approach are easier to check. Data formats are used to describe tensors, not operations/graph, lets put the description close to what it is describing for easiness of checking.

Best wishes,
Tian Jin.

Adding my 2 cents (with regards to Baidu's PaddlePaddle).

Firstly, I think ONNX offers an important IR for the case of training too. I have no doubts there; I am not even thinking long term - this is already a need with things like n-Graph. Yes, I understand there is work needed to fully support that. 

I originally was of the favor of having ops for the backward pass within the ONNX graph, but talking more to my team, it doesn't like such a good idea anymore.

Forward pass IRs from frameworks to runtimes should be the only information runtimes actually need. If hardware makers want optimizations for the backward pass, and they are designing backend runtimes to run the forward pass, than it must be onto them on how they build the backward pass from the information they have from the IR's forward pass description.

1. The unexpected token error is exactly that: The token isn't expected, because the reconstructor doesn't know about the `!` operator, and assumes they would be filtered away. You have to tell it that it's not the case (look at the `rule.options` object)

2. For reconstruction, we can treat `?rule` the same way we treat `_rule`. It's essentially the same, but only applies when `len(expansion) == 1`. And since we're using a parser to reconstruct the parsed tree, it will match it only if it's relevant (there's a chance for ambiguity, but that's another story).

3. Yes, it's very slow. Using LALR could make it much faster, but I don't know if LALR is strong enough to reconstruct the Python grammar. This is all very new research, and I don't think anyone ever attempted it before, so I don't have any answers!

If you want to improve the speed, the first thing I would try is to cache to reconstruction parsers. Right now we're building them over and over for each branch of the parsed tree.

This is related to what I am trying to achieve... It still is quite hypothetical but let's suppose : 
- I define a grammar in a .lark file. Lets say it s a lambda based programming language...

- I define a Transformer in python. For example something that interpret lambda expressions to python. Note, there could also be multiple layers of it, one reducing from s-expr to normal form ("compilation") and second translating from normal-form to python for interpretation...

- Then I would like to, somehow, be able to : 
```python
import anyport.lark_importer
with anyport.lark_importer(
    grammar='mylang',
    module_extension='mylang',
    parser='lalr',
    transformer=TransformChain()
):
    from . import module
```
It should find both `mylang.lark` and `module.mylang` from the current directory.
And upon import my `module.mylang` will be executed, by python (via the chain of transformers).

I think this would make it quite easy to embed DSLs into python...

While writing this it seems the importer behavior might somehow be linked to what the TransformChain is trying to achieve (compilation to bytecode, rewriting of file, etc.).
So probably the interface of the importer will be quite different in the end, and match more the interface of [importlib's SourceLoader](https://docs.python.org/3/library/importlib.html#importlib.abc.SourceLoader).
